speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of the interview of survivor Jack Arnel on August 29, 1996. Mr. Arnel, you were just telling us about the roundups in the streets of Vilna of Jews who were wearing yellow badges, who were then taken to Ponary and executed. Did you know anyone, or were any of your relatives involved in any of these roundups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were. There were so many different roundups every-- practically every week or every other day at that time, in the beginning, especially. They rounded up-- they wanted to diminish the Jewish population, and there were some of my cousins that were caught. We didn't know at the time that they were being killed, you know? We thought that they were taken to somewheres to work. But after-- after these few months, the Germans have announced that actually what happened was they cleared a certain section in the center if Vilna was a-- where mostly Jewish people lived. They took all the inhabitants out of this section, and they disappeared. They took them away. They took him to the Lukiskes prison from where we found out later that they were all taken to Ponary and shot dead over there. And so this area that they cleared of inhabitants, they also had some Polish people had to move out of there, and they formed the ghetto. Actually, they formed two ghettos next to each other where they-- one street, the [INAUDIBLE] Street, which is actually translated German street, in between as a border dividing the ghetto number one and ghetto number two. And then the order came that all the Jews from the entire city of Vilna must move to the ghetto, to the designated area, and they can only take with them only the things that they can carry with them. You're not allowed to have any-- any horse and wagon or any kind of other transportation. Only what you can carry on your back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recollect when this order took place what the date was, what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I believe that it had to be, let's see, April, June-- I would imagine about September. To be exact, September 6, 1941. September 6, 1941 was the day when the ghetto was established, and all the Jews had to go. Now, the tragedy was so great, all of a sudden we walked out of our-- of our apartment, and we left all our possessions there. The Polish neighbors of ours, we had given them away some things rather than leave it there. They stood there crying, a lot of them, bemoaning our fate, and some of them didn't show at all, were hiding in their apartments. But when we walked out of the gate, we noticed a stream of people walking with bundles on their backs. Mothers holding onto children, and fathers, and everybody, the tears rolling down their-- their eyes. And-- and there were a lot of Polish, I guess, and Lithuanian hooligans who would actually jump at some of the people and grab their bundles away at a time like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of them were sickly and could not carry this heavy load, and would drop to the street, and open up, and the contents spilled out. It was a scene that has made a very, very great impact on me. I just was walking around with a big pain in my heart. Couldn't understand it, couldn't understand it. I used to turn around with tears in my eyes to my mother and my father. I say, why? What have we done wrong? I was only 12 years old, you know? It was very difficult to take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, these columns-- after column, after column, from all different districts of the city, they all converged into that area where they designated the ghetto. All the windows that were facing towards the outside was boarded up. Doors were boarded up. In between the streets where it ended, they put brick walls up, and they enclosed everything. And all the population was so crowded we had to share two families into one little room. That's how crowded it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they assign you to these apartments, or you just took what was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to-- those that came in first were able to find a better spot. Those that came in from farther distances [INAUDIBLE] some people couldn't find a place. They had to stay on the stairs, and in the hallways, and on top of the bundles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of the building that you actually went to live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I couldn't remember the address because we were there a short time. I just want to mention this to you, that the people have-- by the time the ghettos were established, out of 65,000 people, 25,000 were already slaughtered in Ponary. They're talking about around 30,000 to 35,000 people that actually moved into the ghetto. My father and us, we came into the ghetto, and even though we were in the ghetto then, he still had to go to work. The Germans gave him special permission. They give him a special-- they called it a [INAUDIBLE] or a-- a certificate of employment. Him and the other furriers had to continue to go to the fur factory and do the work while we were-- remained waiting for him. He used to be marched back and forth in the morning to the factory and in the evening come back. And the Germans had discovered that it is too cumbersome for the furriers to keep marching back and forth like that, and so they obtained permission to form a small camp, a labor camp, outside of the ghetto, like three miles-- two or three miles away from the ghetto near the factory, and it was called Kailis. Actually, the factory was also Kailis. Kailis is a Lithuanian word that has to do with furs. I think it's fur, or fur is kailis. And so they designated the name of Kailis to that labor camp, and there were actually two big buildings, six or seven story buildings, next to each other, plus some-- a few other houses next to it that was all fenced in all around. And they closed off a gate with an entrance in there, and they formed some police among the Jewish people, as well as on the outside was the German watchmen. And they took all of the Jewish furriers-- not all, but for the most part-- into this place called Kailis labor camp. Kailis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this Kailis labor camp was primarily a fur factory? Is that what they did there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly a fur factory, right. All they did was work on the fur coats and fur vests. We had-- later on, we still had a lot of connections with the ghetto because a lot of furriers were still living in the ghetto. There was not enough room for them in Kailis, and they had to still march back and forth to their work of place-- the place of work, and so we used to get all the news from the ghetto as time went on. Every so often, [INAUDIBLE] actions, and they would take Jews away, 1,500, 2,000 Jews. First men, and then women and children. They used to take them, supposedly sent to another sent to another camp where they would do some work. People still didn't know that Ponary existed, that Jews are being taken to Lukiskes, and from there, they were sent. We in turn were placed in Kailis. And we were imprisoned, and we had rations with food that they gave us, which was very small. Life became a-- daily news that came in became almost a frightening experience every single day, because every single day, we got these reports from what is happening in the ghetto, how they kept still taking people to the slaughter. And we thought that any morning, any day that'll come, they'll come and get us just as well. We were trapped, and we were helpless. And the fear of death, we faced death every single moment of every single day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father's nephews that had worked with him in the first shop, did they also come to Kailis as furriers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, two of them were caught in the roundup of Jews and killed in Ponary. None of them made it to the Kailis. They were killed before the establishment of Kailis. But we continued to live there. Some of the elder people tried to get some semblance of-- of diversion, so to divert our minds a little bit. We had a little-- formed a little class for the children secretly where they taught us a little math, a little literature, and very quietly we sang some little songs. And we tried to pass the time away like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the teacher was who ran that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember, but they were all killed, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably-- not at the moment. I don't remember. But they-- they tried to make the children feel a litter better, to get their mind off the fear and the helplessness that we have [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that work for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It worked somewhat, yes. It helped for an evening, if we got a little bit at that moment of the dangers, and the threat, and-- and all the killing and all the-- what was going on in the ghetto and all that, it was good to have a little bit of a diversion. And that's what they intended to do, especially for the young. We had-- there was a time in 1944. I don't know exactly. Excuse me, in 19-- 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see if we can get the dates right. You're talking about the Vilna ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm talking about that one point, before they-- actually, the Vilna ghetto at one point was liquidated. That was in 1940. I believe it was June of 1943. At that time, gradually--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we didn't really get to that point yet in-- in the evolution of your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is part of what happened, see. The people in Kailis remained after the liquidation of the ghetto The ghetto was liquidated in June of 1943, and Kailis maintained until June of 1944. Remained, rather, there. Now, what happened there, at one point the Germans made an announcement in Kailis that all the mothers and the children are to be taken. Across the street was some kind of a hospital, a clinic, over there. We have to be taken there to be inoculated against typhoid-- against typhoid fever. Typhoid And the men had gone to work, and those who had little babies and those that were up to the age of 15 at the time have to go there. So we all went there, Including myself. As we came there, as everybody came there, there was another labor camp near us, a smaller of not furriers, but they were mechanics. They used to refurbish trucks and fixed trucks, and it was called HKP. I don't recall exactly what the letters stood for right now. But their children also had to be brought there, and everybody came in there to this hospital, suddenly out of nowhere, the Germans and the Lithuanians came, and they surrounded the entire hospital with machine guns, and stormed in-- into the hospital where all the people were there. And they pulled trucks-- a line of trucks backed up into the doorway, and they started grabbing the children and throwing them onto the trucks. The Lithuanians especially were very brutal. And the mothers started screaming and yelling, and some of the mothers were begging the Nazis to allow them to escort their children. A few are allowed, and some were beaten over their head with-- with the rifle butts turned-- put aside. And I was also pushed onto a truck, but there were also some elderly people in wheelchairs at the same time, too, that had to come with the children for this inoculation, supposedly. And one German, a Gestapo guy, looks at me, and he says, you! He says, get off the truck and go. You, and you, and another kid, he says, you go and carry the old people, the invalids, in their wheelchairs, and put them on the truck. And so we had to jump off the truck, and go and carry those people, load them onto the trucks. And when all the trucks were loaded up, and everybody was gone, they sent some of the mothers back to the camp, and they kept us yet a little bit longer. And then they made a decision. He says, you two, you're strong and you can still work. And he said, you go back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was you and the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was me and the other kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Katz-- ?] was-- I don't remember. I don't remember. [? Shlomo Katz, ?] something like that, you know. And my best friend was in this action. His name was Boria Trahtenberg. And he wound up with this transport, and they took all these children to the train, loaded them onto a boxcar, and they all were taken to Auschwitz. I remained with my-- with the other kid, and both of us were returned to the camp, just the two out of the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children would you say were loaded on these trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 250 maybe 220. I'm not sure about the exact count, but that's about where it was. Plus the elderly, plus the invalids [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said your friend was on this truck also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend was on the truck. He was taken away with all-- with all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boria Trahtenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, later on, after the war, I discovered that he-- there was a small, little window in the boxcar, and they had this barbed wire on it. He was able to separate the barbed wire as the train was en route. He didn't know exactly where he was or what it was, but he was able to remove this barbed wire. And at night when there was a little lull and the train slowed down quite a bit, he jumped out the window somewhere in Poland. And he was caught by the police, by the Polish police, and he was handed over to the Germans. And they questioned him, and he said that he-- he fell out of the car or whatever excuse he used, and they sent him to Auschwitz also. And here is what happened. All the others that were taken, they were cremated in the crematoria. He, because of the fact that he jumped out of the train, became a political prisoner. They gave him this classification. And as a political prisoner, they kept him alive. They didn't send him to the crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came back into the camp, and I saw this mass of people confronting me, facing me. Ashen, people were tearing at their hair. Mothers were-- were beating on themselves, and begging with me and pleading with me, where is my child? I says, I don't know. They took them on the truck. And they were actually demanding answers from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the tragedy was so great that I actually collapsed over there, and my mother caught me and took me away. It was-- the scene was horrendous. It was-- it was unbelievable. All these mothers that have lost their children, and I guess they knew pretty much the fate of the children. These was not-- people not-- these were not taken to a labor camp. These was taken to be murdered, to be killed. It was very difficult to go on living afterwards. I and the other kid was-- there were a few children that were hidden, actually, there as well, and they survived it because the Germans actually looked to try to find out the children. But afterwards, they had to be in hiding all the time. When there was a roll call, they had to be put away somewheres to be hidden because there were not supposed to be any children left in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about the fact that you were chosen somehow to survive at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- how did I feel? Of course, I was glad to be back, definitely, but I still wondered in my-- at a young age like that, I still couldn't believe that something so horrendous-- that people by the thousands and thousands are being slaughtered like that. I had such an anger in me. I-- I had feelings of escaping, of running. I didn't know what to think. It was so terrible, so horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did those other mothers continue after that first day to talk to you and ask you questions about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. They tried to ask me questions. They tried to. They were constantly grieving, constantly walking with tears rolling down their-- their cheeks. My friend's parents, Mr. and Mrs. Trahtenberg, I think she-- she went off her mind, I think, because of the fact that it was her only child. And they were quite well-to-do before the war, and they had good connections with some Polish people. And some of them asked them to come, and they would hide them and all that. And they thought that they will perhaps, eventually, do that. But once they lost their only child, Boris, they lost the entire feeling for living. They gave up. They did not want to continue to live. She walked around constantly, his mother, with his shirt, the last shirt that he wore, clutched up against her chest. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] [DRINKS] It was a time of perhaps it was better to die right then and there. The insult, the bestiality, the tragedies that we had to live with every single moment is beyond, really, understanding. Beyond even-- I don't even know how the heck some people endured it. There were cases of suicide. It was incomprehensible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back for a second to the day that you had to leave your apartment and go to the ghetto, I just wonder if any of your Polish friends who lived in the-- the children, I mean-- said anything to you when you were leaving or spoke to you when you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from memory, I think that they were kept back by their parents. See, you must understand that the German-- the Nazi machinery has put a tremendous fear and fright into the Polish, and the Lithuanian residents as well, and they made a proclamation that anybody helping the Jews, hiding [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] be caught, they will be punished in the most severe way, and that meant death. There were some cases like this. So any one of the Gentiles who would have had to risk his life and the life of his family as well, to-- to help like this. The Lithuanians in particular were very, very brutal. They somehow in the thousands were very willing, and they were very helpful to the Germans. They probably thought that [INAUDIBLE] is going to be theirs. They were promised that Lithuania will be an independent country, and towards that end, they did everything they could. But you see, the population-- the Polish and the Lithuanian population-- under the German occupation were very hostile to be the Jews for the most part, and it was impossible for the Jews to hide. Only-- only very few incidents where they were able to find hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did not want risk their lives, and they didn't give a damn at that point for the Jews. It was-- they just didn't extend themselves to a helping hand, even to allow sometimes people to hide for a short time so they can make it to the woods, to the partisans, you know? And things like that. It was-- there was nowheres to turn to. There was nowheres to go. Did-- did Father David have any communication with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Father David-- this is I wanted to tell you-- Father David from the Russian church, somehow he found out-- I guess he surmised that Kailis was where all the furriers were, and he knew that my father was a furrier. We had times-- there were times where people would be walking on the street. And of course, we had boarded up-- our windows were boarded up, but we had certain openings there, little places where we could look out. And my mother noticed one day that Father Davis was across the street marching-- walking back and forth in his robes. And so, he probably was looking for us, and my mother made a motion to him. Somehow she-- and he came across, and it was lull, a moment, where there were no watchmen at that time. And he made contact with-- with us, and he asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Arnel, I'm going to stop you here. This is the end of tape three of the interview of Jack Arnel.
